bull_thesis:
Teleperformance (TEP.PA): a cash-rich, mispriced franchise handed a clean restructuring mandate. The market is pricing extinction; the cash flow says otherwise.

The bull case does not rest on the artificial-intelligence story being true on its own terms. It rests on a simpler asymmetry: a company with more than €10 billion in annual revenue is generating real, hard cash while being valued as though the business is failing. Meanwhile, a board-backed leadership change has handed an incoming chief executive — an AI specialist recruited from McKinsey — a clean slate to defend margins, divest the struggling specialty division, and reset cash returns to shareholders. Three discrete, datable catalysts. You are not paying for growth; you are paying a depressed price for an income and deep-value opportunity where the downside is anchored by cash and the upside is a re-rating away from a sentiment trough.

The most quotable evidence for the bull is, paradoxically, a line from a sell-side sceptic: Deutsche Bank's Ben Wild told management directly that 'your valuation is implying an existential trajectory for the group over the midterm.' When a stock prices existential failure for a business that just raised its dividend and generated a record half-year of cash, the gap between price and reality is the trade.

Pillar 1 — The cash is real, disclosed, and rising. This is the one part of the story that is hard, management-stated, and confirmed on the earnings call rather than promised for the future. Management reported record cash generation in the second half of 2025 — more than €640 million — and the board proposed raising the dividend from €4.20 to €4.50 per share, a 7.1% increase. Even the 2026 outlook, which the bear frames as a cut, calls for €800–850 million of free cash flow (cash left after capital spending) on a roughly €10 billion revenue base. The language around the 2026 forecast — an outgoing CFO who repeatedly hedged 'to be on the safe side... there is uncertainty' — reads like a conservative sandbag from a team clearing the decks before handover. He was explicit that there was 'nothing either in terms of working capital or capital expenditure or tax to be paid' driving the decline — no structural cause. A company throwing off this much cash and raising its payout is not on an existential trajectory, and that is the central mispricing.

Pillar 2 — The core business is resilient and the Americas division has turned positive. Strip the headlines and the operating engine is stable. Core Services — customer care, sales, and back-office processing, the bulk of revenue — grew 2.7% on a like-for-like basis (stripping out currency moves and inflation), which management fairly called 'a stable growth anchor.' The Americas division, which had been in slight decline earlier, swung to plus 1.4% like-for-like on strong performance in India and Latin America. Europe, the Middle East and Africa printed close to 4% growth. The client mix — public sector, consumer goods, financial services and insurance — is a genuine structural buffer. The headline number of minus 0.7% that the bear cites is entirely driven by a weaker US dollar; in constant currency and excluding hyperinflationary economies, the group grew 1.3%. This is a flat-but-stable cash compounder being priced as a business in terminal decline.

Pillar 3 — The struggling division is known, ring-fenced, and still profitable. The decline of more than 9% in Specialized Services is real, but it stems from one identifiable event: the non-renewal of a major Visa services contract. Management stated the decisive fact the bear glosses over — strip out the Visa impact and Specialized Services showed slight positive like-for-like growth. Despite the volume hit, profitability held: there was 'only a slight decrease of this highly attractive business,' showing genuine cost flexibility. The Visa loss is now cycling out of the comparison period, which means the worst of the drag is mechanically behind, not ahead. This is exactly the kind of ring-fenced wound that a strategic portfolio review exists to address.

Pillar 4 — The catalyst: a clean-slate chief executive with a mandate to restructure the portfolio and reset cash returns. This is a catalyst signal precisely because the corporate setup, not the operating quarter, is the driver. The board did not merely change a leader; it handed an incoming chief executive a pre-loaded restructuring agenda. Incoming CEO Jorge Amar announced 'a comprehensive strategic portfolio review of the group' — language the debate dossier correctly reads as code for divestitures and, or, a capital-return reset in 2026. On shareholder returns, the most important forward statement on the call was not a refusal but a clear signal: 'The Board has acknowledged the request from shareholders also for an increased return, and we look into this.' Shareholders are explicitly asking for more cash back. The board has formally acknowledged it. The decision is deferred to a chief executive who takes the seat in weeks. For a deep-value investor, 'we are looking into increased shareholder return' plus €800 million of free cash flow plus a portfolio review equals a credible buyback or special-return catalyst. Two new board members — one with Gulf connections, one with South African roots — reinforce this: the board is being re-pointed toward Gulf capital and the EMEA delivery base that is already growing at roughly 4%.

Pillar 5 — Management is candid, not promotional, about the near-term. A classic value-trap warning sign is hiding bad news while overselling the good. Teleperformance does the opposite. They pre-warned fourth-quarter softness and are explicitly pre-warning that the first quarter of 2026 'will be below the annual guidance.' That candour makes the conservative free-cash-flow outlook more likely to be beatable and de-risks the next earnings release: a first-quarter miss is already in the price because management put it there.

Probability-weighted payoff. Roughly 35% probability — bull case: the portfolio review divests or fixes the specialty division, the board converts 'we are looking into increased return' into a real buyback or special dividend, Core Services holds around 2.7% growth, and the stock re-rates off an 'existential' multiple toward a normalised cash-yield multiple. The starting point is a sentiment trough, so the move would be large. Roughly 40% probability — muddle-through: no divestiture, 0–2% growth, flat 14.6% margin, but the dividend (€4.50, rising) and roughly €800 million of free cash flow still pay you to hold, and the orphaned-guidance overhang lifts once Amar speaks; modest positive total return carried by income. Roughly 25% probability — the bear case materialises (see below). The asymmetry favours the long because the downside is cushioned by a hard, rising cash return while the upside is a re-rating from a price that already assumes existential failure.

Conditions that must hold. First, Core Services must stay positive — a break below zero breaks the floor. Second, free cash flow must land near the €800–850 million outlook; a material miss converts the 'conservative sandbag' read into a 'structural leak' read and kills the cash thesis. Third, Amar's first strategic reveal must produce a concrete capital-return or divestiture decision; if 'we are looking into this' stays words for several quarters, the catalyst decays into a value trap. Fourth, the dividend must be maintained or grown — any cut would be the fastest single confirmation of the bear case. The bull case is, at its core, this: you are being paid real, rising cash to hold an option on a board-mandated restructuring, bought at a price that assumes the company is dying.
bear_thesis:
Teleperformance (TEP.PA): an AI narrative bolted onto a structurally decelerating outsourcer, underwritten by no independent financial data, a complete senior-leadership exodus, and orphaned forward guidance — where the very technology being sold as the growth story is already cannibalising the revenue.

The bear case is the higher-probability reading because it requires nothing to be promised — it relies only on what was disclosed. Every load-bearing element of the bull case is future-tense and unverifiable: a new chief executive who would not speak, €100 million of unproven cost savings, a portfolio review with no announced outcome, a capital return the board 'is looking into.' Every present-tense, disclosed fact is soft. Reported revenue is negative. The 2026 outlook calls for 0–2% growth with a flat margin and lower cash flow. The specialty division is down more than 9%, and analysts believe it is still falling. Artificial intelligence is eating a Teleperformance revenue line today. A crippling evidentiary problem runs through everything: the screener supplied no financial data — no revenue series, no margin history, no earnings-per-share, no free-cash-flow trajectory, no earnings-beat rate. You are being asked to commit real capital to a chief-executive and AI pivot 'with essentially no independent financials in hand.' That alone caps any reasonable confidence level.

Pillar 1 — The business is stalling, and management's own outlook confirms it. Strip the currency adjustments and the headline an investor actually receives is negative: revenue minus 0.7% as reported. The 1.3% like-for-like figure requires excluding the effect of hyperinflationary economies and stripping out currency — the honest translation is flat-to-down revenue with defended margins. Forward, it gets worse by management's own hand: 2026 growth is guided 0–2%, the EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortisation) margin is guided flat at 14.6%, and free cash flow is guided down to €800–850 million from a higher 2025 figure — a no-growth profile with declining cash. Management also volunteers that even this low bar will be undershot near term: 'we anticipate for Q1 a revenue development, which will be below the annual guidance.' A 0–2% top line with a flat margin and a first quarter that misses even that is not a turnaround. It is a stall.

Pillar 2 — The AI story is a defensive narrative with no backward proof, and the numbers do not move the ship. The entire re-rating thesis hinges on 'Future Forward' and 'TP.ai,' yet not a single euro of realised AI benefit appears in the 2025 numbers. The €100 million efficiency programme is 'targeted to be over €100 million in 2026' — a forward target. The 'more than 500 AI projects' is a project count, not a financial yield. €100 million on a roughly €8.5 billion cost base is approximately 1.2 points of cost savings — yet the 2026 margin is guided dead flat at 14.6%, which means the promised savings are entirely consumed by wage inflation, price erosion, or reinvestment. The savings story and the flat-margin outlook are in open contradiction. And the growth-y lines being 'called out for the first time' — AI data services at roughly 7% of group revenue, back-office processing growing at double-digit rates — are too small to matter at the group level: 7% of revenue growing at high-single-digit adds well under half a percentage point to group growth. The story is bigger than its contribution.

Pillar 3 — AI is already cannibalising Teleperformance's own revenue. This is the most damaging structural fact in the file and directly refutes the bull's 'AI equals upside' framing. Trust and Safety revenue fell from roughly 10% to roughly 8% of group revenue year-on-year, and management confirmed the cause when pressed by an analyst: 'there is some automation happening... it's also linked to increased automation and AI improvements in that space.' AI is cutting a Teleperformance revenue line right now, even as Teleperformance sells AI as its growth future. The same force is both the pitch and the threat. The €100 million of AI cost savings — a cost-line benefit, still only promised — arrives after the AI disruption to the revenue line, which is already here. That sequencing undermines any long thesis built on AI as an upside option.

Pillar 4 — Maximum governance risk: a complete senior-leadership exodus and orphaned forward guidance. Founder and CEO Daniel, Deputy CEO Mackenbrock, and Chief Financial Officer Rigaudy all depart on 15 March — 'Daniel, myself and Olivier will step down a day before,' and 'Daniel will also step down from the Board.' Losing the founder, the CFO, and the deputy simultaneously is peak continuity risk. The deeper problem is what the dossier calls 'orphaned guidance': the people who set the 2025 targets and are issuing the 2026 outlook are walking out three weeks later, while the man who must deliver 2026 — Jorge Amar — 'did not set them and did not speak to them.' Amar explicitly declined to engage: 'Today is not the day to speak at length.' The 2026 numbers are set by a departing team, to be executed by an unproven one. Guidance credibility rests on continuity of the people who own it; here the owners are gone. Calling Amar a 'McKinsey AI expert' is an asset, but a consultant with no operating profit-and-loss track record at this company, who would not articulate a single strategic commitment on day one, is an untested bet — not a catalyst.

Pillar 5 — The specialty decline is deeper than admitted, and management will not quantify it. The bull says 'strip out Visa and it grew' — but an analyst at UBS dismantled that: 'given the Visa exit should be fully annualised... the implication is probably that the language services situation is still deteriorating.' With the one-off Visa loss already in the comparison base, continued weakness means the underlying language and interpretation franchise is weakening independently. Management's response was a non-answer — 'we don't call out, in particular, the development on language services' — while redirecting to press coverage and adding that 'it was not such an easy start for language services this year.' The diagnostic tell is the CFO's unprompted denial: 'it's far from being a collapse... we are not in disaster mode, far from it.' Management does not say 'far from a collapse' about a healthy line. The need to pre-empt the word 'collapse' is itself the signal. They concede direction — down — while withholding magnitude.

Pillar 6 — The management team dodges precisely where the investment verdict lives. The bull's entire catalyst is capital returns and a portfolio fix; on both, management deflected. Deutsche Bank asked directly how they weigh organic spending versus acquisitions versus 'returning the significant cash to your shareholders,' and the deputy CEO punted entirely: 'with the new CEO coming in, I cannot say more than what we have written... I don't want to preempt any decisions being made by the new management.' Shareholders are explicitly asking for more capital back. The stock is priced for existential failure. And management defers the entire question to a chief executive who has not started. The two things equity holders most need — capital-return policy and the true depth of the specialty decline — are precisely the two things management declined to address.
consensus_delta:
Where we disagree with the market — and how large is that gap really?

The single most important data point about market perception comes from the sell side itself: Deutsche Bank's Ben Wild told management to its face that 'your valuation is implying an existential trajectory for the group over the midterm.' That is the consensus — Teleperformance priced for terminal decline, a melting outsourcing business being disintermediated by the very AI it is trying to sell. The bull's entire edge is that this framing is factually too dark. A company with €10 billion in revenue that generated more than €640 million of second-half free cash flow and raised its dividend from €4.20 to €4.50 — a 7.1% increase — is, by definition, not on an existential trajectory. The market has extrapolated the minus 9% specialty collapse and the AI-cannibalisaton fear across the whole franchise, when roughly 93% of the group — Core Services, growing 2.7% in like-for-like terms, with the Americas recovering to plus 1.4% and Europe, the Middle East and Africa running at roughly 4% — is stable to growing and demonstrably cash-generative.

However, the gap between the market's view and reality is narrow, not wide, because the street's specific, checkable assumptions are mostly correct. No growth: management's own 2026 outlook is 0–2%, with the first quarter explicitly guided below that. Flat profitability: the margin guide is an unchanged 14.6%. Declining cash: 2026 free cash flow is guided down to €800–850 million from a higher 2025 figure, by management's own hand. The specialty division still weakening after the Visa loss: an analyst reasoned that with Visa now cycling out of the comparison, continued weakness 'implies the language services situation is still deteriorating,' and management's non-answer effectively conceded it. The street is not wrong on the fundamentals — it is wrong only on the terminal or extinction framing.

The current interest-rate environment tightens this gap further rather than widening it. The re-rating the bull needs is a yield and value re-rating — paying a higher multiple for roughly €800 million of free cash flow and a dividend yield above 4%. But with central bank rates on hold at elevated levels, the discount rate that values that dividend stream is pinned high, which mechanically caps the re-rating. A 4% dividend yield is far less special when cash and short-dated government bonds pay roughly 5%; the 'cash floor' that anchors the bull is a thinner cushion in a high-rate world than it was when interest rates were near zero. The regime does not invalidate the gap — it shrinks the size of the prize on the one axis (terminal re-rating) where the street is actually wrong, while leaving intact every near-term assumption where the street is right.

Crucially, the thesis does not depend on a central-bank rate cut. It is a self-help governance and portfolio story — a governance reset already executed, a strategic portfolio review now open, a capital-return decision pending — so it survives the screen against rate-rescue-dependent theses. And its resolution driver is genuinely independent of the crowded shared market factors — the re-rating turns on idiosyncratic board action, not on the broader artificial-intelligence capital-spending trade.

The net: the gap with the market is real but asymmetric. The street is too pessimistic on the long-term franchise value (extinction is wrong; the cash flow refutes it) but approximately correct on near-term earnings power (stall is right; management confirms it). The current rate environment caps how much the terminal mispricing can pay. The next two earnings releases — a first quarter below the already-low guide, and free cash flow tracking lower — will confirm the bearish data, not refute it. This is an arbitrage on a change in perception, not on a beat of the numbers — and it must be sized as such, especially with no independent financial data available to calibrate the gap.
forcing_function:
The catalyst that forces the re-rating — and why its lack of a fixed date keeps the verdict at 'wait for the wash-out' rather than 'buy now.'

The re-rating catalyst is real, identifiable, and entirely at management's discretion with no announced schedule. The near-term headwinds, by contrast, are concrete and on the calendar.

Calendared events (mostly negative). By 15–16 March 2026 — now already past as of the date of this debate — founder and CEO Daniel, Deputy CEO Mackenbrock, and CFO Rigaudy stepped down, and Jorge Amar (the McKinsey global customer-service and AI practice leader) became chief executive. The known leadership break is behind us; the awaited positive catalyst is still ahead and still undated. Late April or early May 2026 — also likely now past — first-quarter 2026 revenue, pre-announced by management as below even the 0–2% full-year guide. If that landed as guided, it confirmed the bear's near-term read while doing nothing to date the positive catalyst. Around July or August 2026 — inside the debate's preferred window — the first-half 2026 results: the first independent read on whether the free-cash-flow outlook was a conservative sandbag or a real structural leak, and whether the language services division is still deteriorating with the Visa comparison now fully annualised. This is the one calendared event in the window, but it is skewed toward confirming the bear (on cash and language services) before it can confirm the bull, and it is a results release, not necessarily the strategic or capital-return reveal.

The true catalyst — real, but no date. The event that can actually shatter the 'existential' consensus is the new chief executive's first strategic update or capital-markets day, at which Amar must convert two deferred questions into decisions. First: the outcome of the 'comprehensive strategic portfolio review' — a concrete divestiture of the struggling specialty and language services lines. Second: the capital-return reset — converting 'The Board has acknowledged the request from shareholders for an increased return, and we look into this' into an actual buyback or special distribution. A credible divestiture-plus-buyback package is precisely the kind of catalyst — governance reset already done via the board refresh, strategic process already open via the portfolio review, sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately) and capital return being the missing third leg — that would force the multiple off an extinction level.

Why the current rate environment sharpens the 'wait' verdict. Three judgements converge. First, the positive catalyst has no announced date, and the debate framework explicitly penalises that — it rewards a hard-dated catalyst inside the first or second half of 2026 window and we have a catalyst that is rich in potential but has no clock, while the negative prints do have a clock and have largely already fired. Second, the narrowing regulatory and deal-making window after the November 2026 mid-term elections gives the new chief executive and the board a genuine incentive to act on the portfolio review and capital-return reset inside the first or second half of 2026 rather than defer into a tighter 2027 environment. That narrowing window is, unusually for a special situation like this, a mild forcing tailwind on the very catalyst that is otherwise undated. Third, the thesis does not need a rate cut to work — it is self-help — so it passes cleanly through that screen and sits in a favoured category of distressed or restructuring situations in a thinly covered European market.

Why the verdict is 'wait for the wash-out,' not 'buy now.' An aggressive entry requires the catalyst to be imminent and dated; this one is contingent on a new chief executive choosing to speak, sometime in 2026, after prints that confirm the bear. The disciplined posture is to watch and let the calendared negatives clear — the first quarter below guidance (likely now past) and the first-half free-cash-flow and language-services read around July or August — and enter on the earlier of: (a) the new chief executive formally scheduling a capital-markets day or announcing a concrete portfolio-review divestiture or buyback, converting the catalyst into a dated event inside the preferred window; or (b) a capitulation sell-off on a soft first-half print that does not break the dividend, where the price clears but the cash floor holds. The fastest single invalidation to monitor: any dividend cut or rebase — in the current elevated-rate environment it would simultaneously confirm 'structural leak,' force a second mechanical wave of selling from income holders who now have a roughly 5% risk-free alternative, and remove the only hard floor under the name.
valley_of_death:
What goes wrong in the next three to nine months — the risky window before a positive catalyst can arrive.

This is the strongest argument for watching rather than buying aggressively: the entire near-term tape is pre-loaded to print bad news before anything can print good, and the current macroeconomic environment adds new pressure directly relevant to this business.

The first-quarter 2026 miss was pre-announced. Management volunteered that 'we anticipate for Q1 a revenue development, which will be below the annual guidance' — below even 0–2%. The first datable event after the February earnings call (first-quarter 2026 revenue, around late April or early May 2026) was a guaranteed soft print into a tape already pricing existential decline. With equity market positioning stretched and the marginal dollar unforgiving toward a no-growth, founder-exiting, AI-disrupted European outsourcer, a soft first quarter landing three weeks into an unproven chief executive's tenure with no offsetting strategic reveal is maximally bad sequencing.

An oil and wage-inflation shock hits the cost line directly. Teleperformance is a people-heavy outsourcer with approximately €8.5 billion in annual costs. The overwhelming majority of those costs are wages across Latin America, India, and the Europe, the Middle East and Africa region. A sustained oil and inflation shock — sticky consumer price inflation at around 3.8%, elevated oil prices — is precisely the macro that re-accelerates wage inflation in those delivery geographies and pressures the flat 14.6% margin the bull needs to hold. The €100 million AI savings target is a 2026 promise with no 2025 realisation; if those savings are already being consumed by wage inflation in a relatively benign environment, as the flat-margin guide implies, a re-accelerating inflation environment makes the margin guide harder, not easier, to hit.

The specialty and language services division is the next negative print. With the Visa comparison now cycling out of the base, the year-on-year comparison gets easier on the headline but harder to interpret: any continued weakness in Specialized Services can no longer be attributed to Visa and is, as an analyst at UBS reasoned, direct evidence that the language services franchise is 'still deteriorating.' The CFO's unprompted 'far from a collapse, not in disaster mode' is the tell that the magnitude of the decline is being withheld. This is a live risk with a one-to-two quarter fuse.

The free-cash-flow 'conservative sandbag versus real leak' question resolves the wrong way first. Free cash flow is guided down roughly 9% to €800–850 million. The bull needs this to be a floor the new team beats — but that read can only be vindicated late (a 2026 free-cash-flow beat reported in early 2027), whereas it can be falsified early (first-half 2026 cash conversion tracking low, reported around July or August 2026). The asymmetry of proof runs against the holder in the three-to-nine month window: the bad news arrives fast, the good news arrives slowly. In the current elevated-rate environment, a free-cash-flow or dividend wobble is punished harder because the dividend is the primary reason a marginal holder owns this name.

AI cannibalisaton is a continuous headwind and does not pause. Trust and Safety fell from roughly 10% to roughly 8% of group revenue due to client automation. That erosion does not wait for the €100 million of promised cost savings to arrive. The AI disruption to revenue is real-time; the AI benefit to costs is still a promise.

Reported results will look worse than operations due to currency. The minus 0.7% reported figure versus plus 1.3% like-for-like was dollar-driven; the 2026 margin guide is struck at a USD/EUR rate of 1.20. Reported-versus-like-for-like drift keeps the optics negative through 2026 earnings releases regardless of what is actually happening in the underlying business.

What is not in the risky window — the mitigants. There is no disclosed debt maturity wall, no covenant cliff, and no cash-burn risk. The business is free-cash-flow-positive (roughly €800 million even in the guided-down year) and the dividend is funded and raised. This is a sentiment-and-earnings-prints window — first quarter below guide, language services unquantified, free cash flow unproven, Trust and Safety eroding, compounded by a potential wage-cost squeeze — with no scheduled positive catalyst to interrupt it. The downside is cushioned by the cash floor and the dividend, but the current high-rate environment thins that cushion, and the path to any catalyst runs entirely downhill first. A textbook 'wait for the flush,' not 'step in front of it.'
positioning_washout:
Is there forced, mechanical selling that clears the decks? Partially — and what exists has likely already largely occurred, which is itself a reason to wait. The half of the selling that has not yet happened is more dangerous under the current rate environment, not less.

The shareholder base is split and both halves are under pressure. Teleperformance at an 'existential' valuation — with an implied dividend yield above 4% on a depressed price — has transitioned from its former status as a growth and quality compounder to a deep-value and income holding. That transition forces turnover. Growth and quality-momentum funds that owned Teleperformance as a compounder are structurally wrong-handed on a 0–2%-growth, founder-exiting, AI-disrupted European outsourcer and have strong mandate reasons to have already sold. The governance shock — founder, CFO, and deputy all departing on 15 March — is exactly the event that triggers quality and environmental, social, and governance-governance screens to drop a name mechanically. Much of that exit — growth-holder capitulation on the regime change — is probably already in the tape, given the 'existential' multiple Deutsche Bank describes. In a stretched equity market where a handful of large technology names dominate index performance and AI capital spending drives a significant share of earnings growth, a de-rating European value turnaround story is exactly what momentum and growth mandates are selling to chase the crowded winners, so the growth-holder exit is if anything accelerated.

The unfinished forced-selling pressure — and the tail risk that inverts the bull. The incoming holder class is yield and value buyers anchored on the €4.50 dividend and more than €800 million of free cash flow. This base is stable only as long as the dividend is. That is the un-sprung positioning trap, and the current rate environment sharpens it two ways. First, with risk-free rates at roughly 5%, a dividend yield just above 4% with cut risk is a weak hold for a marginal income buyer, who is more likely to exit on the first free-cash-flow wobble than in a low-rate world. Second, the oil and wage-cost squeeze raises the genuine probability that the new chief executive's strategic review concludes the €100 million AI programme requires capital-expenditure-heavy reinvestment — at which point a dividend rebase becomes thinkable. A dividend cut would force the current income and value base to liquidate mechanically (income funds cannot hold a dividend cutter), producing a second, sharper wave of selling from the very buyers the bull relies on to hold the floor.

The picture is not 'selling complete, decks cleared.' It is 'growth holders mostly gone, income holders in place but conditional on a dividend that is itself guided into a year of minus 9% free cash flow, held by hands made weaker by a 5% risk-free alternative.' Correct posture: wait for the specific event that either re-confirms the dividend and capital-return commitment under the new chief executive — removing the income-base tail risk and validating the floor — or triggers the second wave of selling (a dividend rebase or disappointing strategic review) that finally creates the strong-handed, fully-flushed entry point. Until one of those resolves, you are buying into a shareholder base that can still mechanically sell, in a rate environment that incentivises exactly that.
moderator_conclusion:
Synthesis — Teleperformance (TEP.PA). Verdict: wait for the wash-out. Conviction: 3 of 5.

This is the rare case where the bull and the bear are both substantially right, and the disagreement is purely about timing and provability. The bull is correct on the terminal point: a franchise generating more than €640 million of second-half free cash flow and raising its dividend is not an extinction event, and Deutsche Bank's own line — 'your valuation is implying an existential trajectory' — is the tell that the market has over-extrapolated a minus 9%, ring-fenced specialty wound across a roughly 93%-stable, cash-generative core. The bear is correct on everything checkable and near-term: reported revenue is negative, 2026 is guided 0–2% with a flat 14.6% margin and free cash flow down 9%, the first quarter is pre-announced below even that guide, the specialty and language services division is — per a well-reasoned UBS question that management did not refute — still deteriorating with the Visa comparison annualised, artificial intelligence is actively cannibalising the Trust and Safety revenue line today while the €100 million of AI cost savings remains a 2026 promise with no 2025 proof, and the founder, CFO, and deputy all exited on 15 March leaving orphaned guidance for an unproven chief executive who declined to commit to anything on the call.

The current rate environment cuts both ways, netting to 'hold the line at conviction 3.' Headwinds pushing toward a pass: elevated central-bank rates cap the terminal re-rating that is the bull's only real edge — a dividend yield just above 4% is a thin reward and a weak magnet for holders when risk-free pays roughly 5%; an unresolved oil and inflation shock hits a labour-intensive outsourcer directly on the wage line, putting the bull's required flat-margin guide at risk; stretched equity market positioning makes the tape unforgiving toward a no-growth European turnaround story on any soft print; and the lack of a fixed date on the positive catalyst is penalised in a framework that rewards hard-dated catalysts inside the first or second half of the year — every calendared event is negative, the leadership handover and the pre-warned first-quarter miss are now past, and we are inside the information vacuum with the bad prints landing first. Mitigants holding it above a pass: the thesis requires no rate cut — it is pure self-help, governance reset already executed, portfolio review already open, capital-return reset pending — so it passes the screen against rate-rescue-dependent theses cleanly; it sits in a favoured category of spinoff and divestiture restructuring situations in a thinly covered European market; its resolution driver is genuinely independent of the crowded shared market factors, which is prized when many theses hinge on the same drivers; and the narrowing regulatory and deal-making window after November 2026 gives the board a real incentive to convert the portfolio review and buyback inside the year. The headwinds and mitigants roughly offset: the current environment makes the downside thinner-cushioned and the undated catalyst a heavier demerit, but the self-help independence and favoured-lane fit prevent a drop to a pass.

The decisive judgement — sequencing and evidence. On sequencing: every calendared event in the relevant window is skewed negative (leadership handover past; first quarter below guide, likely now past; first-half free-cash-flow and language-services read around July or August, inside the window but bear-skewed), while the only event that can shatter the bearish consensus — Amar's portfolio-review outcome and capital-return reset — is at his discretion and has no announced date. That alone disqualifies an aggressive entry. On evidence: the screener supplied no financial data. There is no revenue series, no margin history, no free-cash-flow trajectory, no earnings-beat rate to work with. The debate dossier flags this three times. A capital-allocation decision on a chief-executive and AI pivot with essentially no independent financials available, into a rate environment that thins the cushion, cannot be given conviction 4 or 5; the evidentiary gap is a hard ceiling.

Why conviction 3, not 2. This is not a pure value trap to pass. There is a genuine, identifiable catalyst structure — a board-sanctioned governance reset already executed (including a director with Gulf sovereign-capital connections), an opened strategic portfolio review (language the dossier correctly reads as code for divestitures), and a formally acknowledged shareholder demand for increased capital returns — sitting on top of a hard cash floor (funded, rising dividend; roughly €800 million of free cash flow even in the guided-down year) that genuinely cushions the downside. It is self-help and independent in its resolution driver, in a favoured restructuring lane, with the narrowing deal-making window as a real incentive to act in the year. That is more than a stable hold and more than a sell; it is a real but unscheduled catalyst whose timing is adverse and whose terminal prize the high-rate environment caps. Management credibility 3 of 5; trajectory turning; moat narrow but real; moat trend stable; secular threat manageable.

Why conviction 3, not 4. The prior internal conviction of 4 looks a notch too generous given the orphaned guidance and the complete absence of verifiable financials in this packet, and the current rate environment independently argues against 4. A conviction of 4 — meaning 'watch closely with a clear entry timing' — requires the catalyst to have a clock; this one does not. The near-term tape is pre-loaded negative and largely already printing, and elevated rates cap the re-rating that a 4 would be underwriting. The two positives — self-help independence and the favoured-lane fit with the narrowing-window incentive — hold it at 3 rather than dropping it below; they do not earn a promotion to 4 while the catalyst remains undated and the financials remain absent.

Timing — the actionable part of the 'wait for the wash-out' verdict. Do not initiate into the calendared misses; the leadership handover and the first-quarter below-guide print are already behind us, and the first-half free-cash-flow and language-services read is the next bear-skewed print. Watch and enter on the earlier of two specific triggers: (a) the new chief executive formally scheduling a capital-markets day or strategic update, or announcing a concrete portfolio-review divestiture or buyback — the catalyst acquires a date inside the preferred first-to-second-half 2026 window, at which point re-rate toward 'buy now' and size up; the narrowing post-mid-term deal-making window makes this more probable in 2026 than in a normal cycle; or (b) a capitulation sell-off on a soft first-half 2026 print that does not break the dividend — forced income and quality sellers clear, the cash floor proves intact at a discount, and you buy the wash-out. The fastest single invalidation to monitor: any dividend cut or rebase. In the current elevated-rate environment it would simultaneously confirm 'structural leak,' force a second mechanical wave of selling from an income base that now has a roughly 5% risk-free alternative, and remove the only hard floor under the name. Net: a real franchise priced too darkly, with a real, self-help, rate-independent catalyst that is real but unscheduled, underwritten by no independent financials, into an environment that caps the terminal prize and squeezes the wage-cost margin — but sitting in a favoured restructuring lane with a narrowing deal-making window incentive to act in the year. A textbook 'wait for the wash-out' at conviction 3 — own it as a starter or option position only once the new chief executive puts a date on the re-rating inside the window, or once the flush gives you the cash floor at a discount.
interrogator_dossier:
Forensic Dossier — Teleperformance SE (TEP.PA)

Sector: Communication and IT Services (Customer Experience and business-process outsourcing). Signal type: Catalyst. Latest call: 26 February 2026 (full-year 2025 results).

Transcripts reviewed: one call only — the full-year 2025 results presentation, hosted by Deputy CEO Thomas Mackenbrock with Chief Financial Officer Olivier Rigaudy and incoming CEO Jorge Amar.

Coverage caveat — read this first. The committee reviewed a single earnings call, and even that transcript was truncated: the mid-call prepared remarks are absent from the source, leaving only the opening highlights and the closing analyst question-and-answer session. The quarter-by-quarter trajectory must therefore be reconstructed from inside one call — management repeatedly back-references its own first-quarter, first-half, and third-quarter 2025 commentary, allowing triangulation, but no earlier calls were independently reviewed. Equally important: the screener supplied no financial data. The raw data block is empty. There is no revenue series, no margin history, no earnings per share, no fair-value estimate, no earnings-beat rate. Section 2 below can only test management's claims against the figures management itself stated on this call and against the prior screener dossier's qualitative read — not against an independent financial trajectory. Where a number is absent, this dossier says so rather than inventing one. This caveat materially caps the achievable credibility score: a story this pivotal, with this little independently verifiable backing, cannot be rated as 'corroborated by financials.'

A note on the name: the company refers to itself as 'TP' throughout; this is Teleperformance SE, ticker TEP.PA, the global leader in outsourced customer experience and business-process outsourcing.


Narrative Arc

Even in one compressed call, the arc is unmistakable: a regime change dressed up as continuity. Three movements run through the full-year 2025 presentation.

Movement 1 — 'We met everything we promised.' The call opens not with strategy but with box-ticking: 'we have met all our updated 2025 objectives.' The load-bearing word is 'updated.' The group is hitting guidance that was revised during the year, not the guidance it originally set. Revenue is a bit over €10 billion; like-for-like growth is plus 1.3% excluding hyperinflationary economies but minus 0.7% as reported, on a weak US dollar; EBITDA is almost €1.5 billion at a 14.6% reported margin; second-half free cash flow is a record more than €640 million; the dividend is raised from €4.20 to €4.50. This is the 'nothing is broken' foundation on which everything else rests.

Movement 2 — The split-screen business: a stable core, a cracking specialty. Management bifurcates the group. Core Services — customer care, sales, back-office processing — is the 'stable growth anchor,' up 2.7% like-for-like, with the Americas swinging from slightly negative earlier in the year to plus 1.4% (India and Latin America cited) and the Europe, the Middle East and Africa region at close to 4%. Specialized Services is the wound: down more than 9% like-for-like, driven by the non-renewal of the significant Visa contract and a soft US market for the language interpretation line. Management's defence is twofold: strip Visa and Specialized Services was 'a slight positive'; and despite the volume hit, 'we have proven to maintain a strong profitability.' Within the portfolio, the growth-oriented lines being called out are AI data services (described for the first time, growing at high-single-digit rates, roughly 7% of group) and back-office processing (double-digit growth). The quietly retreating lines are Trust and Safety — flagged by an analyst as falling from roughly 10% to roughly 8% of group revenue year-on-year — and the language interpretation franchise.

Movement 3 — 'An AI company now, run by an AI person.' This is the headline and the reason the debate labels this a catalyst signal. The founder era ends: Daniel (founder and CEO), Thomas Mackenbrock (Deputy CEO), and Olivier Rigaudy (CFO) all step down on 15 March. Jorge Amar — 'a very world-renowned AI expert and leader of McKinsey's global customer service practice' — becomes CEO on 16 March, taking the founder's board seat. The 'Future Forward' plan (launched summer 2025) is reframed around three levers: accelerate growth, drive efficiency using AI, and transform the company. A concrete target is attached: an AI efficiency programme 'targeted to be over €100 million in 2026,' backed by 'more than 500 AI projects' launched in 2025. Importantly, the new management team also opens 'a comprehensive strategic portfolio review' — signalling possible divestitures.

Net arc: a low-growth, currency-buffeted customer-experience outsourcer is being handed from its founder to a McKinsey AI partner, while simultaneously leaning into an 'AI will grow us and make us efficient' story and putting the whole portfolio under review. The thesis has expanded in ambition while the reported top line is contracting and pieces are being reviewed. That gap — soaring narrative, flat-to-shrinking reported revenue — is the defining tension of this file, and it maps exactly to the prior dossier's read: AI framing as 'a defensive strategy to mask stagnant top-line growth and a complete senior-leadership exodus.'


Claims Versus the Numbers (Forensic)

Methodological warning, which cannot be over-stressed: the screener provided no financial block for TEP.PA. Every number below is sourced from management's own statements on this call or from the prior dossier's qualitative summary — there is no independent financial series to verify management against. Treat this section as 'claims versus management's own stated figures,' not 'claims versus audited screener trajectory.' That is a real and serious limitation for a capital-allocation decision.

'We have met all our updated 2025 objectives — solid delivery.' Revenue roughly €10 billion; plus 1.3% like-for-like excluding hyperinflation, minus 0.7% as reported; EBITDA roughly €1.5 billion at 14.6% margin. Technically true, materially flattering. 'Met' applies to downwardly revised guidance, and the headline an investor actually receives — reported revenue — is negative. The 1.3% figure requires stripping out hyperinflation and currency moves. The honest description is flat-to-down revenue with a defended margin.

'Core Services is a stable growth anchor, plus 2.7% like-for-like; Americas recovered to plus 1.4%, the Europe, the Middle East and Africa region at roughly 4%.' Mostly verified, with a deceleration tell. The 2.7% anchor is credible and is the strongest single pillar. But management volunteers that the fourth quarter softened and the first quarter of 2026 starts below even the 0–2% full-year range — the anchor is dragging, not accelerating.

'Specialized Services: only a slight decrease in profitability despite the volume hit.' Minus 9% plus on revenue; strip Visa and 'a slight positive.' Half true, with spin risk. Profitability resilience is plausible and management repeats it, but it is unverifiable here — no segment-level margin is disclosed. The minus 9% revenue decline is real and large. An analyst at UBS reasoned that with Visa now annualised, continued weakness implies the language services franchise is still deteriorating independently, and management's non-answer effectively conceded direction while withholding magnitude.

'AI data services growing at high-single-digit, roughly 7% of group; back-office processing at double-digit.' Plausible but immaterial to the whole. Even at 7% of revenue growing at high-single-digit, the contribution to group growth is well under half a percentage point — not enough to move a €10 billion business off its 0–2% trajectory. The story is bigger than the contribution.

'2026 guidance: 0–2% growth, stable 14.6% EBITDA margin at a USD/EUR rate of 1.20, free cash flow €800–850 million.' Weak, and management is honestly candid about it. A 0–2% top line with a flat margin is a no-growth profile. To management's credit, the guide is specific and they flag the first quarter will fall below it — they are not hiding the softness.

'Dividend raised €4.20 to €4.50, plus 7.1%; record second-half free cash flow more than €640 million.' Verified on the call and genuinely supportive. This is the most credible value pillar: cash generation funds a rising dividend. But 2026 free cash flow is guided down to €800–850 million from a higher 2025 figure — management attributes the decline to working-capital and tax timing and conservative assumptions, not a structural cause. Unverifiable from external data, but the divergence is real.

'Future Forward AI programme will deliver more than €100 million of efficiency savings in 2026; more than 500 AI projects launched.' Pure forward promise, zero backward proof. No 2025 realised AI savings are quantified. €100 million on roughly €8.5 billion of costs is approximately 1.2 percentage points of cost savings — yet the 2026 margin is guided flat, implying those savings are being consumed by wage inflation, price erosion, or reinvestment. The savings story and the flat-margin guide are in quiet tension.


Tone and Confidence Trajectory

Rating: declining — from offensive to defensive — masked by upbeat delivery.

With only one call, the trajectory is read within it and through management's own back-references. In the first quarter and first half of 2025, the CFO admits management 'was amazed by the impact of the reduction of the growth we were waiting for' in the specialty division — a genuine forecasting miss. By the third quarter, 'Future Forward' was being introduced and the three strategic levers warmed up a quarter before the leadership change was announced. By the fourth quarter and full-year call — the one reviewed here — the dominant register is defensive, delivered in an upbeat wrapper. Growth and acceleration language exists but is future-tense: 'accelerate the growth,' 'transform the company,' 'scale further.' The present-tense vocabulary is efficiency, cost, adjustment, restructuring, portfolio review, and reacting quickly. The CFO's signature moment is a defence of operational agility: 'the ability of this group to adjust quickly... decisions are taken quickly... we have systems that enable us to detect quickly what is happening on the field and to react if needed.' That is a cost-and-control message, not a revenue-demand message.

The tell-tale denials. Under genuine pressure, confidence tends to show up as negations of bad scenarios: on the language services line, the CFO volunteers 'it is far from being a collapse... we are not in disaster mode, far from it.' Management does not say 'far from a collapse' about a healthy business line. The need to pre-empt the word 'collapse' is itself the signal.

Offensive versus defensive balance: decisively shifted to defensive for everything that is present and measurable — margin defence, cost programmes, restructuring, agility, conservative cash-flow assumptions — with offence reserved for the future and the unmeasurable: AI, growth acceleration, transformation. That asymmetry is the classic profile of a company managing a stall.

Specificity of guidance: mixed. The financial guide is admirably specific — 0–2%, 14.6% margin at 1.20 USD/EUR, €800–850 million free cash flow, €70–90 million one-off restructuring costs, €100 million AI target. But on the two questions that most matter to equity holders — capital-return policy and valuation — the answer is a deferral.


Guidance Credibility

Reliability: medium, trending toward the lower end.

They hit 2025 — but the bar moved. 'Met all our updated 2025 objectives.' Hitting revised guidance is a materially weaker credibility signal than hitting original guidance, and the revision direction — down, mid-year, on specialty — tells us the original 2025 guide was a miss-in-progress that was reset. By the CFO's own admission management was 'amazed' by the first-quarter specialty deterioration — forecasting was caught out early in the year.

They pre-warned fourth-quarter weakness and are pre-warning the first quarter of 2026 will print below the 0–2% range. That is honest expectation management and a credibility positive. They are not setting investors up for a surprise.

Possible conservative sandbagging on free cash flow — but unproven. The 2026 free-cash-flow guide (€800–850 million, down roughly 9% year-on-year) is defended as conservatism: working-capital and tax timing and 'to be on the safe side... there is uncertainty.' This could be deliberate low-balling by an outgoing CFO clearing the decks for the new chief executive — a classic kitchen-sink move before a handover. But it is equally consistent with a real structural cash drag (wage-cost reinvestment, front-loaded restructuring spend), and there are no independent financials to distinguish the two. The guide was set by a departing team, to be executed by an unproven one. That structurally caps reliability at medium regardless of how specific the numbers are.


Analyst Pressure Points

Even in the truncated question-and-answer session, analysts converge on the soft spots, and management's non-answers are diagnostic.

The capital-return and valuation question (Deutsche Bank, Ben Wild) — fully deflected. Wild's framing is direct and worth quoting: 'your valuation is implying an existential trajectory for the group over the midterm.' He asks directly how management weighs organic operating expenditure and capital expenditure versus acquisitions versus 'returning the significant cash to your shareholders.' Mackenbrock punts entirely: 'with the new CEO coming in, I cannot say more than what we have written... The Board has acknowledged the request from shareholders also for an increased return, and we look into this... I don't want to preempt any decisions being made by the new management.' This is the single most important non-answer on the call. Shareholders are explicitly asking for more capital back. The stock is priced for an existential outcome. And management defers the entire question to a chief executive who has not started. The market's central worry — value trap or cash-return story? — is left open.

Language services and specialty trajectory (UBS, Nicole Manion) — soft-deflected. Manion correctly reasons that with the Visa exit fully annualised, continued weakness 'implies the language services situation is still deteriorating.' Management 'don't call out, in particular, the development on language services' and redirect to press coverage, softened by the CFO's 'far from a collapse.' They concede direction while withholding magnitude. This is the topic most likely to become the next negative print.

Trust and Safety erosion (UBS) — answered, and it is structural. Manion flags the drop from roughly 10% to roughly 8% of group. Management confirms it is client automation and AI disruption: 'there is some automation happening... linked to increased automation and AI improvements in that space.' AI is cutting a Teleperformance revenue line right now, even as Teleperformance sells AI as its growth future. The same force is both the pitch and the threat.

The free-cash-flow gap versus operating profit (Deutsche Bank) — answered defensively. Why is free cash flow guided down roughly 9% when operating profit is roughly flat? Answer: working-capital and tax timing (Americas cash conversion) and conservatism. Plausible, not independently checkable.

Restructuring front-loading (UBS) — answered honestly. Roughly €56 million of the €70–90 million one-off restructuring budget was already earmarked, confirming a heavy, immediate people-cost restructuring.

Pattern: management is forthcoming on operations and costs, evasive on the two things equity holders most need — capital-return policy and the true depth of the specialty decline. The deflections are concentrated exactly where the investment verdict lives.


Red Flags and Green Flags

Red flags.

Complete senior-leadership and founder exit, simultaneously. Founder and CEO Daniel, Deputy CEO Mackenbrock, and CFO Rigaudy all depart 15 March. Losing the founder, the CFO, and the deputy in one stroke is maximum continuity risk, however well-telegraphed.

Orphaned guidance. The 2026 outlook is set by people leaving, owned by no one who stays. Amar pointedly declined to speak to strategy or numbers.

Reported revenue is negative (minus 0.7%) and 2026 is guided 0–2% with a flat margin and lower free cash flow. Stripped of currency and hyperinflation adjustments, this is a stalled, no-growth profile.

Specialized Services down more than 9% with the underlying language services line analysts believe is still deteriorating after the Visa comparison cycles out, and management declining to quantify the magnitude.

AI is actively cannibalising Teleperformance's own Trust and Safety revenue (from roughly 10% to roughly 8% of group) via client automation — the disruption is arriving on the revenue line before the promised €100 million of AI savings shows up on the cost line.

The Deutsche Bank 'existential trajectory' remark — a sell-side analyst openly stating the valuation implies an existential outcome is a clear signal about how the market is pricing the franchise.

'Met all our updated objectives' — guidance was cut mid-year; hitting the lowered bar is being presented as a win.

Green flags.

Cash generation and a raised dividend (€4.20 to €4.50, plus 7.1%; record second-half free cash flow more than €640 million). The income and value foundation is intact, and management is returning more, not less.

Core Services is genuinely resilient (plus 2.7% like-for-like; Americas recovered to plus 1.4%, the Europe, the Middle East and Africa region at roughly 4%), and client diversification across public sector, consumer goods, and financial services and insurance is a real structural buffer.

Candour on near-term weakness. Management pre-warned fourth-quarter softness and is pre-warning the first quarter of 2026 will fall below the annual guide — no sandbag-then-surprise on the downside.

A strategic portfolio review under new leadership — genuine optionality to divest the structurally challenged specialty lines and re-rate. A real potential catalyst if executed.

Specialty profitability defended despite a minus 9% volume hit (management's claim; unverified but repeated and specific) — shows operational cost flexibility.


Hidden Signals

The 'TP' rebrand vocabulary. Management exclusively says 'TP' and 'TP.ai,' never the full 'Teleperformance.' A founder-era industrial-services name is being quietly retired in favour of a short, technology-flavoured mark — cosmetic signalling that the company's identity is being repositioned from outsourcer to AI and tech, ahead of any change in the actual revenue mix.

'We call this out for the first time.' Management is unbundling the few growth-oriented lines — AI data services at roughly 7%, back-office processing at double-digit — to give investors something fresh to underwrite, while re-bundling the shrinking ones. An analyst at UBS caught the reverse manoeuvre: Trust and Safety reporting was reorganised such that it 'was split between other and Trust and Safety.' Disclosure is being reorganised to surface growth and submerge decline. Watch the segment definitions closely under the new chief executive.

The board's geographic re-weighting. Two new directors — one with Gulf connections, one from South Africa. The Gulf connection is relevant to the capital-return and 'increased return' question shareholders are pressing; South Africa maps to the Europe, the Middle East and Africa delivery footprint management praises. The board is being re-pointed toward Gulf capital and EMEA operations.

'Strategic portfolio review' is deal-making language. Coupled with Mackenbrock's repeated refusal to 'preempt' the new chief executive on capital allocation, this strongly signals divestitures and, or, a capital-return reset are coming in 2026 — the new management team is being handed a clean mandate to restructure the portfolio. This is the most important forward-looking signal: the next catalyst is a portfolio and capital-return decision, not an operating beat.

Amar's deliberate silence is itself a message. A new chief executive who is a customer-service and AI specialist, declining to articulate strategy on day one, means the real strategy reveal is a future event — likely a capital-markets day. Today's call was a handover, not a plan.

The CFO's 'amazed' admission about first-quarter specialty weakness is a rare unguarded look at forecasting fallibility — a quiet acknowledgement that management's own visibility into the specialty franchise was poor at the start of 2025.


Capital-Allocation Verdict

Classification: turning — not strengthening, not merely deteriorating. This is a deliberate, board-sanctioned regime change underway: new chief executive, AI-led strategy, and an explicit strategic portfolio review. The operating trajectory is soft and decelerating; the corporate trajectory is a hard turn. Both are true simultaneously.

The single most important thing to weigh before sizing a position: this is a bet on an unproven chief executive and an unwritten plan, underwritten by no independent financials. Every load-bearing element of the bull case is future and unverifiable — the McKinsey AI expert who has not started and would not speak, the €100 million of AI savings with no 2025 proof, the 'strategic portfolio review' with no announced outcome, the 'increased shareholder return' the board 'is looking into' but will not commit to. Against that, every present and disclosed fact is soft: minus 0.7% reported revenue, a 0–2% guide that the first quarter will undershoot, a flat margin, free cash flow guided down 9%, a minus 9% specialty division analysts believe is still falling, and AI eating the Trust and Safety line today. The one solid anchor is cash: real free cash flow and a raised dividend make this defensible as an income and deep-value, catalyst-driven option on a successful pivot — not as a growth holding.

For sizing: this is a catalyst trade contingent on the new chief executive's first strategic reveal and the portfolio-review and capital-return decision, both pending and neither assessable today. Until Amar speaks and the portfolio review produces an outcome, the asymmetry is narrative-up, evidence-down. Size it as a starter or option position with a hard catalyst date — the new chief executive's strategy update or next capital-markets event — not as a conviction core holding. The prior internal conviction of 4 looks a notch too generous given the orphaned guidance and the complete absence of verifiable financials in this packet; treat the cash-return floor as the only thing currently underwriting the downside. Management credibility 3 of 5; trajectory turning; moat narrow but real; moat trend stable; secular threat manageable.

bull_thesis: Teleperformance is the global #1 in CX/BPO trading at ~3.8-4.5x EV/EBITDA and ~2.8x P/FCF — a multiple pricing terminal decline into a business that still produced ~€1.5B EBITDA and ~€900M FCF in a 'bad' FY-25 (€640M+ in H2 alone), pays a RISING dividend (€4.20→€4.50, ~7% yield at €63) and runs a buyback. The market extrapolates the shrinking 14-15% (Specialized Services: LanguageLine + the lost UK visa/TLS contract) onto the whole company, while the ~85% Core Services BPO mass accelerated through 2025 (LFL 2.3%→3.5%, EMEA/APAC ~5-6%, improved retention). Three re-rate legs: (1) GOVERNANCE RESET — founder Julien + co-CEO Mackenbrock + CFO Rigaudy all exited 15-Mar-2026 for outsider CEO Jorge Amar (ex-McKinsey global CX/AI lead), the profile installed to restructure/crystallize value; (2) an explicit 'comprehensive STRATEGIC PORTFOLIO REVIEW including potential asset sales' with the board 'acknowledging shareholder requests for increased return' — coded SoP/breakup language; Deutsche Bank notes Specialized's niche assets carry 'very high valuation benchmarks in private and public markets' (9-12x to a strategic vs ~4x in TP's stub); (3) a €100M+ AI-efficiency program funded by €70-90M restructuring protecting margins as the mix verticalizes toward stickier back-office/BPO and AI-data work (both double-digit/high-single-digit, FY-25). Peer comps frame the dislocation: Concentrix ~8-10x FCF, TaskUs ~12x; the SCALE LEADER at 2.8x is a >60% discount. Holds only if Core re-stabilizes (it dipped -1.7% LFL in Q1-26 — needs the promised H2-26 reaccel), the review converts to a divestiture/larger return within ~12 months, and AI proves a margin tailwind not a volume cliff. A single niche-asset sale at a private multiple exposes Core's implied stub as absurdly low and closes the gap toward €81-86.
bear_thesis: A structurally AI-disrupted commodity labor-arbitrage business dressed as deep value, and the newest data shows disruption reaching the CORE. The cheapness sits on a DECLINING base: NI fell every year (€643M→€497M), EPS 10.77→8.40, FCF -26% YoY, and the '€1B sustainable FCF' anchor (FY-24) is ALREADY broken in the FY-26 €800-850M guide. Decisive new evidence: in Q1-2026 (28-Apr-2026) CORE SERVICES went NEGATIVE at -1.7% LFL and group -2.2% LFL — the 'resilient' 85% rolled over on 'offshoring, Trust & Safety automation, geopolitical ramp delays.' Trust & Safety shrank ~10%→8% of revenue on explicit client-side automation — hard proof AI REMOVES TP revenue, falsifying the vanished 2024 'Gen-AI is our friend' case studies. Specialized is in accelerating decline (-7%→-12% in 2025, -5.5% Q1-26) with NO recovery date and zero segment disclosure. Leverage went the WRONG way — ~€4.0B net debt after the $490M ZP deal while buying back stock, Altman-Z 2.8 (grey), Piotroski 6/9; there is NO net-cash cushion (the input's 'NEGATIVE=net cash' note is an ERROR). The catalyst is SOFT/UNDATED: at Q1-26 CEO Amar offered only 'very interesting conversations… nothing firm I can report,' no timeline; a CEO 12 weeks into the seat is incentivized to kitchen-sink and study, and Morgan Stanley CUT its target ~50% on the upheaval, explicitly WAITING for a strategic update before crediting review upside. Entire institutional memory left on one day at the most fragile moment, with insiders buying NOTHING (ratio 0.00, smart-money 19/100). Confirmation: H2-26 fails to reaccelerate, the review ends in 'we'll optimize' not a sale, LLS keeps bleeding on US immigration policy, FCF steps down again — and 2.8x P/FCF re-rates not up but to a permanent melting-ice-cube multiple, revisiting the €45.50 low.
consensus_delta: DEEP-VALUE framing: the street is split, not uniformly wrong, so the expectations-arbitrage edge is thin. The CHEAP read (consensus target €85.70, ~35% above €63; our SoP €72) says the market prices terminal decline into a leader still throwing off ~€800M+ FCF — that part is correct and is the genuine mispricing. But the SKEPTICAL read is now data-supported and is the one the tape is acting on: the assumption the bull most relies upon — that the 85% Core is 'resilient/accelerating' — was FALSIFIED in Q1-2026 when Core went -1.7% LFL and group -2.2% LFL, below the maintained 0-2% guide, on AI-automation + offshoring reaching the core. So the precise incorrect street assumption is NOT 'TP is cheap' (it is) but 'Core Services is a stable growth anchor immune to AI' — that anchor cracked, and Trust & Safety shrinking 10%→8% on client automation is the proof. The expectations gap that remains is whether the portfolio review monetizes the niche Specialized assets at private multiples the public stub ignores — but with no transaction and no date, that is an unpriced OPTION, not a knowable delta. Net: the consensus is converging toward the bears in real time (MS -50% target cut), which removes the 'street looking the wrong way' asymmetry that a deep-value entry requires.
forcing_function: No HARD dated forcing function exists — the defining weakness. The candidate events: (1) Q2-2026 / H1-2026 results (~late-July/early-August 2026) — the first real test of the 'H2 reacceleration' promise and whether Core re-crosses to positive LFL; a miss is a downside forcing event, not an upside one. (2) Any interim strategic-review milestone or a 2026 Capital Markets Day under Amar — but management has explicitly given NO timeline ('nothing firm to report,' Q1-26), so this is a soft, slippable window, not a binding date. (3) An actual divestiture announcement of a Specialized Services niche asset (ZP / AI-data / LLS) at a private-market multiple — THE event that would crystallize the SoP and re-rate the stub — but none is announced and none is dated. (4) The late-May AGM (dividend approval) is a non-event for the thesis. Because the genuine re-rating driver (review→transaction) is undated and discretionary to a new CEO, there is nothing to underwrite a near-term catalyst around; you are buying an open-ended option on management action.
valley_of_death: Concrete near-term (3-9mo) hazards before any re-rate: (1) H1-2026 is guided SOFT by management's own words ('a softer H1, true acceleration in H2') — and Q1 already missed the annual guide at -2.2% LFL; if Q2 (reported ~late-Jul/Aug-2026) shows Core still negative, the 'H2 reaccel' promise loses all credibility and the stock re-tests €45-50. (2) FCF steps DOWN in FY-26 to €800-850M (−9% YoY) with €70-90M of restructuring cash out the door (€56M already earmarked, front-loaded) — a visibly shrinking cash base is the worst optic for a 'cheap on FCF' thesis. (3) LanguageLine remains hostage to US immigration/ICE policy and weather-sensitive interpretation volume with NO recovery date — an exogenous, un-modelable drag that can deteriorate further. # Teleperformance SE (TEP.PA) — Forensic Interrogator Dossier
*Transcripts: 2024-07-30 (H1-24) · 2025-02-28 (FY-24) · 2025-05-02 (Q1-25) · 2025-08-01 (H1-25) · 2026-02-26 (FY-25). Source: FMP earnings transcripts. Financials: screener scan + FMP TTM as of 2025-12-31.*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story has inverted over five quarters — from "successful consolidator riding AI as a tailwind" to "structurally challenged incumbent in survival-by-efficiency mode under brand-new management."

**Act I (H1-24, FY-24): The Majorel integration & "AI is our friend."** The opening narrative is offensive and acquisitive. Olivier Rigaudy frames H1-24 around "a smooth integration of Majorel," reported growth of +28.2% (Majorel-fuelled), and an evangelical case that "Gen-AI is not absolutely an enemy for TP, absolutely not, it's all absolutely the opposite." Two glossy AI case studies are paraded (UK digital bank, US web-host) to argue AI *grows* headcount-equivalent revenue. Capital allocation is confident: €117M buyback in H1-24, then a €500M buyback program and a dividend hike to €4.20 announced at FY-24, plus the $490M ZP acquisition to bolster Specialized Services. Leverage is being actively de-risked toward "below two times" net-debt/EBITDA, hitting 1.9x by FY-24. The closing FY-24 line is a swagger: "this company is safe, solid and will continue to deliver a solid free cash flow" of "roughly €1 billion a year."

**Act II (Q1-25, H1-25): "A story of two tales" — the bifurcation surfaces.** New co-CEO Thomas Mackenbrock takes the mic and the narrative splits permanently into a healthy **Core Services** (BPO, ~85-86% of revenue) accelerating (+2.3% → +3.5% LFL through 2025) against a deteriorating **Specialized Services** (visa/LanguageLine) that goes from "-7%" ex-visa softness in H1-25 to "almost minus 12% in Q2." The non-renewal of "a significant Visa Application" contract (UK government, TLS) becomes the recurring scapegoat. LanguageLine ("LLS") softness is pinned on the U.S. political environment ("a direct link" to ICE/immigration volume, and even Los Angeles events). Management starts *lowering* the bar: FY-25 guidance is cut to "the lower end" on FX and Specialized weakness. The AI narrative pivots from "we build it" to "we partner" — a flurry of minority stakes (Sanas $13M, Ema, Parloa, Agents Only) and a new "Future Forward / TP.ai FAB" strategy launched at the June-25 Capital Markets Day.

**Act III (FY-25, the most recent call): Governance reset + "strategic portfolio review."** The defining event. Founder/CEO Daniel Julien, co-CEO Mackenbrock, and CFO Rigaudy *all step down simultaneously* on March 15, 2026, replaced by **Jorge Amar** (ex-McKinsey global customer-service/AI practice lead) as new group CEO from March 16. Crucially, management announces "a comprehensive **strategic portfolio review** of the group" and explicitly acknowledges "the request from shareholders also for an increased return" — the language of a company under pressure to crystallize value. The growth narrative has shrunk to a defensive "0% to 2%" 2026 guide with Q1 below even that, a €100M+ AI-efficiency cost program, and €70-90M of restructuring/social-plan costs. The thesis has fully contracted from "leader compounding via M&A" to "shrink-to-margin, review the portfolio, return cash, hand it to a new CEO."

**Drivers introduced then quietly dropped:** Majorel synergy *upside* ("might deliver much more," H1-24) was talked up, then settled to the flat €150M target. The two H1-24 AI growth case studies vanished entirely — replaced by the partnership-ecosystem story. Trust & Safety, once ~10% of revenue, was quietly disclosed at 8% (FY-25) and falling on "automation on our client side" — the first concrete admission that AI is *taking* revenue, not adding it.

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

The hard numbers expose a business whose top line was inflated by an acquisition while underlying earnings power *fell every single year*. Management's "solid, resilient, €1B FCF" refrain is materially richer than the trajectory justifies.

| Management claim | Quarter | The actual financials | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "more than €10 billion revenue… €1.08bn free cash flow… highly resilient and strong business model" | FY-24 | Revenue 2021→2025: 7.12 → 8.15 → 8.35 → **10.28 → 10.21B**. The jump to €10B is **entirely Majorel/ZP M&A**; organic LFL was only +1.3-2.6%, and FY-25 revenue *fell* -0.7% YoY. | **Half-true / flattered by M&A.** Scale bought, not grown. |
| "this company is safe, solid… solid free cash flow of roughly €1 billion a year" | FY-24 | Net income fell every year: 643 → 592 → **523 → 497M**. EPS: 10.77 → 10.01 → 8.71 → **8.40**. FCF growth **-26.1% YoY**; FY-25 FCF guide cut to **€800-850M**. | **Diverges.** The "€1B FCF" anchor is *already broken* in the FY-26 guide (down 9%). Earnings power is in multi-year decline. |
| "margin expansion last year… up 10bps" | FY-24 | Gross margin 11.8% and described as **contracting**; net margin compressed to **4.9%**. EBITDA margin guided *down* to 14.6-14.8% reported for FY-25/26. | **Cherry-picked.** True at constant-FX EBITDA; reported margins are flat-to-down. |
